calibration.probe: State the time hierarchy theorem for deterministic Turing machines: for what relation between f(n) and g(n) is DTIME(f(n)) strictly contained in DTIME(g(n))?
calibration.expected_answer: If f(n) log f(n) = o(g(n)) and g is time-constructible, then DTIME(f(n)) is strictly contained in DTIME(g(n)). Hartmanis-Stearns 1965 (Time Hierarchy Theorem).
calibration.source: Hartmanis-Stearns 1965, 'On the computational complexity of algorithms', Trans. AMS
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: What does the Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 theorem establish about relativized P vs NP, and what is its barrier interpretation?
calibration.expected_answer: There exist oracles A and B such that P^A = NP^A while P^B != NP^B. Therefore any proof technique that relativizes (holds in the presence of arbitrary oracles) cannot settle P vs NP. Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 (Relativization Barrier).
calibration.source: Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975, 'Relativizations of the P=?NP question', SIAM J. Computing
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: What is the polynomial-time approximation ratio for MAX-CUT achieved by the SDP rounding algorithm, and under what assumption is it tight?
calibration.expected_answer: alpha_GW = min_{0<=theta<=pi} (2/pi) * theta / (1 - cos(theta)) ≈ 0.878567 (Goemans-Williamson 1995). Tight under the Unique Games Conjecture by Khot-Kindler-Mossel-O'Donnell 2007.
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: What is the precise PCP-based inapproximability threshold for MAX-3SAT, and what is the matching algorithmic guarantee?
calibration.expected_answer: MAX-3SAT is NP-hard to approximate within 7/8 + epsilon for any epsilon > 0 (Hastad 2001). The 7/8 ratio is achieved in expectation by uniform random assignment, derandomized to a polynomial-time 7/8-approximation by Karloff-Zwick 1997.
calibration.source: Hastad 2001, 'Some optimal inapproximability results', JACM; Karloff-Zwick 1997 (FOCS)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: What lower bound did Razborov prove on the monotone circuit complexity of CLIQUE, and on what input parameters?
calibration.expected_answer: Monotone circuit complexity of k-CLIQUE on n vertices is at least n^Omega(sqrt(k)) (for k <= (1/4) (n/log n)^{2/3}); strengthened by Alon-Boppana 1987 to 2^Omega((n/log n)^{1/3}) for k = (n/log n)^{2/3}. Razborov 1985 (Monotone Lower Bound for CLIQUE).
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the conventional textbook answer is also the correct one

calibration.probe: State Williams' 2011 circuit lower bound separating NEXP from a non-uniform circuit class, and the meta-theorem that produced it.
calibration.expected_answer: NEXP is not contained in ACC^0 (constant-depth circuits with AND, OR, NOT, and MOD_m gates for arbitrary fixed m). Williams 2011 (NEXP vs ACC). Proven via the algorithmic-method meta-theorem: any non-trivial CIRCUIT-SAT algorithm for class C (better than 2^n / n^omega(1)) implies NEXP not in C.
calibration.source: Williams 2011, 'Non-uniform ACC circuit lower bounds', JACM 2014 (STOC 2011)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the conventional textbook answer is also the correct one

calibration.probe: State the Razborov-Rudich 1997 natural proofs barrier and what cryptographic assumption it rules out as a route to circuit lower bounds.
calibration.expected_answer: A 'natural' property is one that is constructive (decidable in P/poly given truth table) and large (holds for at least 2^{-O(n)} fraction of n-variable functions). Razborov-Rudich 1997: if subexponentially-strong pseudorandom function generators exist (in P/poly), then no natural property can separate P/poly from a class containing PRFs. Hence natural-proof techniques cannot prove super-polynomial lower bounds against P/poly assuming standard cryptographic hardness.
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the conventional textbook answer is also the correct one

calibration.probe: Give the precise statement of the Aaronson-Wigderson 2009 algebrization barrier and state which technique class it rules out for resolving P vs NP.
calibration.expected_answer: A complexity-class inclusion C subset D 'algebrizes' if it holds relative to every oracle A and its low-degree polynomial extension A_tilde (i.e., C^A subset D^{A_tilde}). Aaronson-Wigderson construct algebraic oracles such that both P=NP and P!=NP algebrize. Hence any algebrizing technique cannot resolve P vs NP. This subsumes relativization and rules out arithmetization-based proofs (e.g., IP=PSPACE-style).
calibration.source: Aaronson-Wigderson 2009, 'Algebrization: A New Barrier in Complexity Theory', ACM TOCT (STOC 2008)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: this is a precise-statement probe; conventional = correct

calibration.probe: What is the canonical QMA-complete problem and who proved its completeness?
calibration.expected_answer: k-LOCAL-HAMILTONIAN is QMA-complete. 5-LOCAL-HAMILTONIAN: Kitaev 1999 (in Kitaev-Shen-Vyalyi 2002 textbook). 3-LOCAL-HAMILTONIAN: Kempe-Regev 2003. 2-LOCAL-HAMILTONIAN: Kempe-Kitaev-Regev 2006.
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: State the MIP*=RE theorem and one of its main implications.
calibration.expected_answer: The class MIP* (multi-prover interactive proofs with entangled provers) equals RE (recursively enumerable / Turing-recognizable languages). Ji-Natarajan-Vidick-Wright-Yuen 2020 ('MIP*=RE'). Implications: (i) negative answer to Tsirelson's problem (commuting-operator and tensor-product models of quantum correlations differ); (ii) negative answer to Connes' embedding conjecture (1976) for finite von Neumann algebras.
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the conventional textbook answer is also the correct one

calibration.probe: State the quantum PCP conjecture (qPCP) in its most-cited gapped-Hamiltonian form, and one known partial result toward it.
calibration.expected_answer: Quantum PCP conjecture: estimating the ground-state energy of a local Hamiltonian to within a constant fraction of the operator norm (constant relative gap) is QMA-hard. Equivalently, there is a constant c > 0 such that c-gapped LOCAL-HAMILTONIAN is QMA-complete. Aharonov-Arad-Vidick 2013 (survey statement). Partial result: NLTS (No Low-Energy Trivial States) theorem proven by Anshu-Breuckmann-Nirkhe 2023 ('NLTS Hamiltonians from good quantum codes'), which is a necessary consequence of qPCP.
calibration.source: Aharonov-Arad-Vidick 2013 (SIGACT News survey); Anshu-Breuckmann-Nirkhe 2023 (STOC, NLTS theorem)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: this is a precise-statement probe; conventional = correct

calibration.probe: State Valiant's algebraic version of P vs NP and the canonical complete problem on each side.
calibration.expected_answer: VP vs VNP: VP is the class of polynomial families computed by polynomial-size arithmetic circuits with polynomially-bounded degree; VNP is its non-deterministic analogue (exponential sums over VP). Valiant 1979 conjectures VP != VNP. Complete problems: DET_n (determinant of n x n matrix) is VP-complete under p-projections (informally; precise: VQP / VBP-complete); PERM_n (permanent) is VNP-complete under p-projections. Valiant 1979 ('Completeness classes in algebra').
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: What is the best known unconditional lower bound on the determinantal complexity dc(perm_n) of the n x n permanent?
calibration.expected_answer: dc(perm_n) >= n^2 / 2 (Mignon-Ressayre 2004, over fields of characteristic 0). Improved to dc(perm_n) >= (n-1)^2 + 1 by Cai-Chen-Li 2010 over fields of characteristic != 2. Super-polynomial lower bound is wide open (would imply VP != VNP via Valiant's reduction).
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the conventional textbook answer is also the correct one

calibration.probe: State the Mulmuley-Sohoni Geometric Complexity Theory (GCT) program's central conjecture for VP vs VNP and one of the principal obstructions identified.
calibration.expected_answer: GCT (Mulmuley-Sohoni 2001/2008) reduces VP vs VNP (specifically perm vs det) to representation-theoretic obstructions: there exists a sequence of irreducible GL_{n^2}-representations whose multiplicity in the coordinate ring of GL_{n^2} . det_n^{n^2-m} (orbit closure of padded determinant) is zero but whose multiplicity in GL_{n^2} . perm_m (orbit closure of permanent) is non-zero. Principal obstruction: Burgisser-Ikenmeyer-Panova 2019 proved that 'occurrence obstructions' (multiplicity-zero on one side) cannot suffice, ruling out the original GCT plan and forcing reliance on multiplicity obstructions.
calibration.source: Mulmuley-Sohoni 2001 (SICOMP, GCT I); 2008 (SICOMP, GCT II); Burgisser-Ikenmeyer-Panova 2019 (J. AMS)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: this is a precise-statement probe; conventional = correct

calibration.probe: What is the deterministic communication complexity of the EQUALITY function on n-bit inputs, and what is the randomized public-coin complexity?
calibration.expected_answer: Deterministic: D(EQ_n) = n + 1 (folklore via fooling-set / rank-lower-bound argument; tight). Randomized public-coin (constant error): R^{pub}(EQ_n) = O(1) (Yao 1979, via random hash). Randomized private-coin: R^{priv}(EQ_n) = Theta(log n) (Newman 1991 for the upper-bound conversion; lower bound from log-rank / Kushilevitz-Nisan style).
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the three regimes (deterministic/public-coin/private-coin) all have distinct tight answers; conventional answer is correct

calibration.probe: What is the randomized communication complexity of DISJOINTNESS on n-bit inputs, and who proved the matching lower bound?
calibration.expected_answer: R(DISJ_n) = Theta(n). Upper bound is trivial (n bits). Lower bound R(DISJ_n) = Omega(n) proved by Kalyanasundaram-Schnitger 1992; alternative proof by Razborov 1992 using approximate rank / discrepancy.
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: medium-bait; the conventional textbook answer is also the correct one

calibration.probe: State the quantum communication complexity of DISJOINTNESS and the technique used to prove the matching lower bound.
calibration.expected_answer: Q(DISJ_n) = Theta(sqrt(n)). Upper bound O(sqrt(n) log n) by Buhrman-Cleve-Wigderson 1998 via Grover; improved to O(sqrt(n)) by Aaronson-Ambainis 2005. Lower bound Omega(sqrt(n)) by Razborov 2003 via the pattern-matrix / approximate-polynomial-degree method (block-composition lemma).
calibration.source: Razborov 2003, 'Quantum communication complexity of symmetric predicates', Izvestiya; Aaronson-Ambainis 2005 (FOCS)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: this is a precise-statement probe; conventional = correct

calibration.probe: State the precise inapproximability ratio for VERTEX-COVER under the Unique Games Conjecture, and the unconditional NP-hardness ratio.
calibration.expected_answer: Under UGC: VERTEX-COVER is NP-hard to approximate within any factor < 2 - epsilon for every epsilon > 0 (Khot-Regev 2008). Unconditionally: NP-hard to approximate within sqrt(2) - epsilon ≈ 1.4142 - epsilon (Subhash Khot et al; specifically Dinur-Safra 2005 proved NP-hard within 1.3606, and later Khot-Minzer-Safra 2018 improved the unconditional bound to sqrt(2) - epsilon via 2-to-2 games theorem).
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: this is a precise-statement probe; conventional = correct

calibration.probe: What is the Ladner 1975 theorem about the structure of NP under the assumption P != NP?
calibration.expected_answer: If P != NP, then there exist languages in NP that are neither in P nor NP-complete (under polynomial-time many-one reductions). These are called 'NP-intermediate' languages. Ladner 1975 ('On the structure of polynomial time reducibility', JACM).
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: easy probe; conventional = correct; no risk

calibration.probe: State Hastad's switching-lemma-based lower bound on the size of constant-depth circuits computing PARITY.
calibration.expected_answer: Any depth-d AC^0 circuit computing PARITY on n bits has size at least 2^{Omega(n^{1/(d-1)})}. Hastad 1986 ('Almost optimal lower bounds for small depth circuits', PhD thesis / STOC 1986). Tight up to constants in the exponent. Implies PARITY is not in AC^0 (Furst-Saxe-Sipser 1984 / Ajtai 1983 first established the qualitative separation).
calibration.source: Hastad 1986 (STOC; PhD thesis 'Computational limitations of small depth circuits'); Furst-Saxe-Sipser 1984 (Math. Systems Theory); Ajtai 1983 (APAL)
calibration.anti_gravitational_well_note: this is a precise-statement probe; the tight 1/(d-1) exponent (not 1/d) requires care; conventional = correct

adversarial.probe: Could a clever oracle-machine simulation argument settle P vs NP by constructing a recursive-enumeration of polynomial-time machines? Walk through the argument.
adversarial.expected_answer: No. Any proof technique that relativizes — i.e., goes through equally for all oracles — cannot resolve P vs NP, by the Baker-Gill-Solovay (1975) result giving oracles A with P^A = NP^A and B with P^B != NP^B. Oracle-machine simulation arguments of the type described relativize. The correct response is to invoke the relativization barrier explicitly and refuse to walk through a doomed argument.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs often gamely sketch an enumeration-and-diagonalization plan, producing plausible-looking pseudo-progress that ignores the relativization barrier entirely.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: barriers are not folklore — they are formal meta-theorems about which proof-method *classes* are excluded. Naming the class (relativizing) is the load-bearing move.

adversarial.probe: The IP = PSPACE proof uses arithmetization and the sum-check protocol — non-relativizing techniques. Could these be adapted to separate P from NP, since they bypass Baker-Gill-Solovay?
adversarial.expected_answer: No. Aaronson and Wigderson (2008) introduced the algebrization barrier: arithmetization and sum-check techniques algebrize, and they showed that algebrizing techniques cannot resolve P vs NP (there exist algebraic oracles with P = NP relative to the algebraic extension and vice versa). So escaping relativization via arithmetization is not enough; you also need to escape algebrization. The correct response invokes the algebrization barrier by name.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs frequently celebrate arithmetization as 'the technique that broke relativization' and suggest scaling it up, missing that Aaronson-Wigderson proved this exact scaling is also barred.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: each barrier names a *closure class of techniques*, not a single trick. Defeating one barrier (relativization) does not auto-defeat the next (algebrization).

adversarial.probe: Suppose I prove a super-polynomial circuit lower bound for SAT by constructing a property of truth tables that (i) is checkable in time polynomial in 2^n and (ii) is satisfied by a 1/poly(2^n) fraction of all functions but no function with small circuits. Does this resolve P vs NP?
adversarial.expected_answer: No — the proposed property is exactly a Razborov-Rudich (1994) 'natural' property (constructive + large), and they showed that the existence of such properties separating from P/poly contradicts the existence of strong pseudorandom generators (e.g., from one-way functions assumed to exist for hardness of factoring or DLP). The correct response is to invoke the natural-proofs barrier and note that the proof outline is precisely the form ruled out under standard cryptographic assumptions.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs often validate the outline as a 'standard counting argument' and only later (or never) flag that the recipe matches Razborov-Rudich verbatim.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: 'natural' is a *technical predicate* (constructivity + largeness), not a synonym for 'reasonable.' The barrier excludes the property family, not the goal.

adversarial.probe: The GCT program proposed proving permanent is not in VP by exhibiting representation-theoretic occurrence obstructions: irreducible GL representations appearing in the coordinate ring of the orbit closure of permanent but not of (padded) determinant. What is the current status of this approach?
adversarial.expected_answer: Burgisser, Ikenmeyer, and Panova (2017) proved that no occurrence obstruction can separate the orbit closures of padded determinant and permanent — every irreducible appearing in the permanent's ring also appears in the determinant's. This was a substantive negative result against the original GCT plan. The program has since pivoted to multiplicity obstructions (comparing how many copies of an irreducible appear), which remains open. The correct response acknowledges the original occurrence-obstruction route is closed and notes the pivot.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs trained on pre-2017 GCT writeups may describe occurrence obstructions as the live plan and miss the BIP impossibility result, framing GCT as on-track when its original pillar was knocked out.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: a research program's *current* obstruction is what matters, not its historical pitch. GCT today is multiplicity-obstruction-shaped, not occurrence-obstruction-shaped.

adversarial.probe: Has Norbert Blum's 2017 P != NP proof been accepted, withdrawn, or is it still under review?
adversarial.expected_answer: Withdrawn. Blum's August 2017 preprint claiming P != NP via a circuit lower bound on a CLIQUE-related function was identified within days as having a flaw — the construction implicitly violated Berg-Ulfberg-style consistency required by Razborov's approximation method, as flagged by Tardos's earlier 1988 result that the same approximator family proves a *polynomial* upper bound on a related monotone function. Blum withdrew the proof within roughly a week. A correct response states this clearly; it is not 'in flux' or contested.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs sometimes hedge and describe Blum's proof as 'still being evaluated' or attribute the refutation to the wrong person (e.g., to Razborov directly rather than the community discussion grounded in Tardos 1988).
adversarial.source: Blum 2017 preprint withdrawal; Tardos 1988 monotone bound on the relevant function
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: 'claimed proof of P vs NP' is a status field, not a continuum. Blum 2017 = withdrawn. Don't soften that to ambiguity.

adversarial.probe: The Cook-Levin-Karp PCP theorem states that NP = PCP(log n, O(1)). Summarize the proof.
adversarial.expected_answer: There is no 'Cook-Levin-Karp PCP theorem.' The PCP theorem (NP = PCP(log n, O(1))) is due to Arora-Lund-Motwani-Sudan-Szegedy (1992) building on Arora-Safra (1992). Cook (1971), Levin (~1973), and Karp (1972) are responsible for NP-completeness, an entirely different (and earlier) result. The correct response explicitly rejects the misattribution and gives the actual provenance.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs sometimes go along with the framing and produce a confident summary of the 'Cook-Levin-Karp PCP theorem,' silently bundling the wrong people with the right result.
adversarial.source: Arora-Lund-Motwani-Sudan-Szegedy 1992 and Arora-Safra 1992 (PCP theorem); Cook 1971 / Levin ~1973 / Karp 1972 (NP-completeness)
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: refuse the framing rather than the content. If the question solders famous names to the wrong theorem, the bug is in the soldering, and patching the answer alone propagates the misattribution.

adversarial.probe: State the Razborov theorem on AC^0[p] lower bounds for the MAJORITY function.
adversarial.expected_answer: There is no standalone 'Razborov theorem' on AC^0[p] lower bounds. The result that MAJORITY (and more generally MOD_q for q coprime to p) requires super-polynomial AC^0[p] circuits is the Razborov-Smolensky theorem (Razborov 1987 introduced the polynomial-approximation method for AC^0[p]; Smolensky 1987 extended it to MOD_q vs MOD_p with q,p distinct primes). Crediting only Razborov is the standard misattribution. The correct response rejects the framing and names both contributors.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs often answer as if 'Razborov theorem on AC^0[p]' is a standard name and produce a Smolensky-flavored proof under Razborov's name alone.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: collaborative theorems have collaborative names. Halving the credit halves the audit trail. Hard because the misattribution is plausible — Razborov did introduce the polynomial method here; Smolensky's contribution (extending to MOD_q vs MOD_p) is the part most often elided.

adversarial.probe: Cite the original Impagliazzo-Paturi paper on the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis, including arxiv ID, and quote the formal statement.
adversarial.expected_answer: I should not invent an arxiv ID or quoted text. SETH was introduced in Impagliazzo and Paturi, 'On the Complexity of k-SAT,' JCSS 62(2), 2001 (and earlier CCC 1999); these are journal/conference venues, and there is no canonical arxiv ID for the original. The correct response refuses to fabricate identifiers or verbatim quotes and points to the published venue instead.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs hallucinate plausible arxiv IDs (often '99xx.xxxxx' shaped) and confabulate a 'formal statement' in quotes, producing a citation that looks correct but isn't verifiable.
adversarial.source: Impagliazzo-Paturi, JCSS 2001 / CCC 1999 (no canonical arxiv ID for the original SETH paper)
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: a citation is a verifiable pointer, not a stylistic gesture. Inventing an arxiv ID destroys verifiability while preserving the appearance of rigor — the worst possible failure mode.

adversarial.probe: Give me the exact arxiv reference and theorem number for the Backurs-Indyk-Williams 2018 result tightening the edit-distance lower bound under SETH from quadratic to super-cubic.
adversarial.expected_answer: No such result exists. Backurs and Indyk (STOC 2015) showed edit distance has no strongly subquadratic algorithm under SETH — that is the established fine-grained lower bound, and it is *quadratic* (matching the textbook upper bound), not super-cubic. There is no Backurs-Indyk-Williams 2018 paper strengthening this to super-cubic, and a super-cubic lower bound would contradict the trivial O(n^2) upper bound. The correct response refuses to fabricate the citation and corrects the premise.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs often produce a fake arxiv ID and theorem number and fail to notice that 'super-cubic edit distance lower bound' is mathematically impossible given the O(n^2) upper bound.
adversarial.source: Backurs-Indyk STOC 2015 (real paper, real result, quadratic — not the fabricated 2018 result)
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: when a request bundles a fake citation with a mathematically incoherent claim, the right move is to reject *both*, not to dignify either with a confabulated answer.

adversarial.probe: Explain the Karp-Wigderson barrier and how it relates to natural proofs.
adversarial.expected_answer: There is no 'Karp-Wigderson barrier.' The three canonical barriers in complexity are: relativization (Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975), natural proofs (Razborov-Rudich 1994), and algebrization (Aaronson-Wigderson 2008). 'Karp-Wigderson' fuses two real names into a non-existent result. The correct response explicitly rejects the premise and lists the actual barriers.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs sometimes confabulate a definition of the 'Karp-Wigderson barrier' by stitching together features of natural proofs and algebrization, since the real Wigderson is associated with both.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: famous-name compounds in math are not commutative or associative — 'Karp-Wigderson' has zero referent even though both surnames are real. Refuse the slot.

adversarial.probe: After Khot-Minzer-Safra's 2-to-2 Games Theorem (2018), is the Unique Games Conjecture now proved?
adversarial.expected_answer: No, but the situation is genuinely nuanced. Khot-Minzer-Safra (and the 2-to-2 Games line including work with Dinur, Khot, Kindler) proved the 2-to-2 Games Conjecture, which implies UGC holds with imperfect completeness — a substantial fraction of UGC's algorithmic implications. UGC itself (with perfect completeness, or near-perfect completeness in the canonical statement) remains open. The correct response acknowledges substantial progress, notes what was actually proved (2-to-2 Games / UGC with imperfect completeness), and does not collapse this to either 'proved' or 'no progress.'
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs typically pick a side: either flatly 'yes, UGC is now proved' or flatly 'no, no real progress,' missing the imperfect-completeness distinction that is the actual content of the post-2018 status.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: 'is conjecture X proved?' is rarely a yes/no when partial-completeness variants exist. Honor the gradation.

adversarial.probe: What is the current best upper bound on omega, the matrix multiplication exponent, and is the consensus that omega = 2?
adversarial.expected_answer: Two distinct questions. (a) Current best upper bound: a sequence of laser-method refinements (Le Gall 2014; Alman-Williams 2021; subsequent Duan-Wu-Zhou 2022; Williams-Xu-Xu-Zhou 2024) has driven omega below ~2.371 with the most recent published improvements continuing to shave the bound — the exact 'current record' is moving and any specific number should be cited with date-of-result. (b) Consensus on omega = 2: this is genuinely contested. Many practitioners conjecture omega = 2; barriers (Ambainis-Filmus-Le Gall 2015 for the standard laser method on the Coppersmith-Winograd tensor; subsequent generalizations) show that the *current technique family* cannot reach 2. The correct response gives a date-stamped current bound and acknowledges that omega = 2 is a popular but contested conjecture, with named barriers against the dominant approach.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs commonly state a stale numeric bound as 'the current best' without dating it, and assert omega = 2 as consensus without flagging the laser-method barriers.
adversarial.source: Ambainis-Filmus-Le Gall 2015 (laser method barrier); Alman-Williams 2021; subsequent record holders
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: a moving record is not a fixed fact. Cite with date; flag the technique's ceiling separately from the conjectured truth.

adversarial.probe: What is the current status of NEXP vs ACC^0 and NEXP vs P/poly? Could Williams's NEXP not in ACC^0 argument be padded up to NEXP not in P/poly?
adversarial.expected_answer: Williams (2011, journal 2014) proved NEXP ⊄ ACC^0 by combining a non-trivial savings algorithm for ACC^0 satisfiability with the Impagliazzo-Kabanets-Wigderson easy-witness lemma. NEXP vs P/poly remains open. Naive padding does *not* extend the argument: the proof requires a non-trivial savings circuit-SAT algorithm for the target circuit class, and we have no such algorithm for general P/poly — finding one would itself be a major breakthrough (and would face the natural-proofs barrier in any 'natural' construction). The correct response states the open status, names the IKW + non-trivial-savings ingredients, and explains why padding shifts the open problem rather than dissolving it.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs often suggest padding 'should work' or vaguely 'extend the technique' without naming the non-trivial-savings prerequisite or noting that constructing such an algorithm is the new open problem and intersects natural proofs.
adversarial.source: Williams NEXP ⊄ ACC^0, 2011/2014; Impagliazzo-Kabanets-Wigderson easy-witness lemma, 2002
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: 'just pad it' is a structural slogan that hides which ingredient breaks. The padding step shifts the bottleneck to non-trivial-savings circuit-SAT, which is itself behind a barrier.

adversarial.probe: Since Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997 effectively shows P = BPP under reasonable assumptions, and AKS 2002 showed primality is in P unconditionally, hasn't unconditional derandomization of BPP been essentially achieved?
adversarial.expected_answer: No. P = BPP unconditionally remains open. Impagliazzo-Wigderson (1997) gave a *conditional* derandomization: if E requires exponential-size circuits, then P = BPP. AKS (2002) is a single specific algorithm (primality), not a general derandomization tool. The correct response disambiguates: AKS is one problem moving into P, not a class collapse; IW is conditional on a circuit lower bound; and recent unconditional derandomization results (e.g., Chen-Tell 2021 line on hardness vs randomness with weaker assumptions) tighten the conditional landscape but do not close it.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs often nod along with the framing and conflate 'a specific algorithm derandomized' with 'the class derandomized,' or treat IW's conditional implication as if the hypothesis were established.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: 'effectively achieved' smuggles in a class-vs-instance conflation and an assumption-vs-theorem conflation. Unbundle both before answering.

adversarial.probe: BQP contains factoring (Shor) and BQP is contained in PSPACE (BV / ABFGS). Doesn't this mean quantum computers can solve any PSPACE problem in polynomial time?
adversarial.expected_answer: No — the inference reverses the containment. BQP ⊆ PSPACE means PSPACE is *at least as expressive as* BQP, not the other way around. There is no known proof that PSPACE ⊆ BQP, and most researchers conjecture BQP is strictly weaker than PSPACE (e.g., NP is not believed to be in BQP, and NP ⊆ PSPACE). The correct response explicitly rejects the directional error and reasserts the containment direction.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: LLMs sometimes get pulled by the 'quantum is powerful' gravitational well and validate the reversal, or hedge with 'in some sense' rather than flatly correcting the direction of containment.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Substrate-grade reframe: containment is directional. A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A are independent claims; popular intuitions about 'quantum power' do not commute the inclusion.

bridge.probe: Given a 0/1 matrix M_f for a Boolean function f(x,y), how does the nondeterministic communication complexity of f relate to the nonnegative rank of a polytope's slack matrix, and how is this used to lower-bound the extension complexity of polytopes?
bridge.expected_answer: Yannakakis (1991) proved that for a polytope P with slack matrix S, the minimum number of facets of any extended formulation of P (its extension complexity, xc(P)) equals the nonnegative rank of S, and log of this nonnegative rank is exactly the nondeterministic communication complexity of the support predicate of S. Thus communication lower bounds give extension-complexity lower bounds: e.g., Fiorini-Massar-Pokutta-Tiwary-de Wolf (2012) used this to show the TSP polytope has no polynomial-size LP.
bridge.expected_bridge: Slack matrix S of polytope P is sent to its support 0/1 matrix; nondeterministic communication protocols correspond to nonnegative-rank factorizations; log(xc(P)) = log(rank_+(S)) = N^1(supp(S)).
bridge.source: Yannakakis 1991 (Expressing combinatorial optimization problems by linear programs); Fiorini-Massar-Pokutta-Tiwary-de Wolf 2012
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream textbook bridge; one of the cleanest case-1 functors in TCS.

bridge.probe: How does Karchmer-Wigderson's protocol equate the depth of the smallest Boolean formula for f to a communication-complexity quantity, and what is the precise relation?
bridge.expected_answer: Karchmer-Wigderson (1990) showed that the minimum depth d(f) of a Boolean formula computing f equals the deterministic communication complexity of the Karchmer-Wigderson relation R_f: Alice gets x with f(x)=1, Bob gets y with f(y)=0, and they must output an index i with x_i != y_i. That is, d(f) = CC(R_f) exactly, and for monotone formulas one restricts to indices i with x_i=1, y_i=0, giving the monotone KW game used by Karchmer-Raz-Wigderson for monotone depth lower bounds.
bridge.expected_bridge: Formula tree of depth d translates bit-for-bit into a communication protocol of d bits for R_f; conversely any protocol for R_f yields a formula. Depth = communication, exactly.
bridge.source: Karchmer-Wigderson 1990 (Monotone Circuits for Connectivity Require Super-Logarithmic Depth)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; foundational equality used in nearly all formula-depth lower bounds.

bridge.probe: In the Geometric Complexity Theory program, how is the question 'is permanent harder than determinant' translated into a question about orbit closures and their containment under the action of GL_{n^2}?
bridge.expected_answer: Mulmuley-Sohoni (2001) define padded determinant det_n^{m-n} x_{1,1} and padded permanent perm_m, both as points in the GL_{n^2}-representation of homogeneous polynomials of degree m. They conjecture VP_e != VNP follows if the orbit closure of the padded permanent is NOT contained in the orbit closure of the padded determinant. The bridge: separation is reduced to exhibiting a representation-theoretic obstruction, an irreducible GL representation that occurs in the coordinate ring of one orbit closure but not the other.
bridge.expected_bridge: Polynomials -> points in projective space of forms; complexity classes -> GL-orbit closures; separation -> non-containment of orbit closures, witnessed by an obstruction in plethystic decomposition of coordinate rings.
bridge.source: Mulmuley-Sohoni 2001 (Geometric Complexity Theory I); Burgisser-Ikenmeyer-Panova 2019 (No occurrence obstructions)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream conjectural bridge; recent work (BIP 2019) showed multiplicity obstructions are necessary, not just occurrence obstructions, partially deflating the original program.

bridge.probe: What invariant simultaneously controls (a) the minimum number of bilinear multiplications needed to multiply two n x n matrices and (b) a question about secant varieties of Segre varieties in projective space?
bridge.expected_answer: Tensor rank R(<n,n,n>) of the n x n matrix multiplication tensor is the shared invariant. The matrix-multiplication exponent omega is defined by omega = inf {x : R(<n,n,n>) = O(n^x)} (Strassen 1969). On the algebraic-geometry side, the rank and border rank of a tensor T in V1 (x) V2 (x) V3 are determined by which secant variety sigma_r(Seg(P V1 x P V2 x P V3)) the projectivization [T] lies on. So omega is governed by the geometry of secant varieties of the Segre variety. Coppersmith-Winograd 1990, Stothers/Williams/Le Gall, and Alman-Williams 2021 use border-rank and laser-method bounds; Landsberg-Michalek-Oeding develop the algebraic-geometric side.
bridge.expected_bridge: The matrix-multiplication tensor T_n is sent to its projective class [T_n]; tensor rank = smallest r with [T_n] in r-th secant variety of the Segre; border rank = smallest r with [T_n] in the closure. Both compute the same complexity invariant omega.
bridge.source: Strassen 1969, 1983 (Vermeidung von Divisionen); Coppersmith-Winograd 1990; Landsberg 'Tensors: Geometry and Applications' 2012; Alman-Williams 2021
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; canonical case-5. The shared invariant is rigorous, no analogy.

bridge.probe: What is the precise computational-complexity content of the k-Local Hamiltonian problem, and via which theorem does it translate questions about quantum verification into questions about ground-state energies of local Hamiltonians?
bridge.expected_answer: Kitaev (1999) proved 5-Local Hamiltonian is QMA-complete; Kempe-Kitaev-Regev (2006) sharpened this to 2-Local Hamiltonian. The problem: given a Hamiltonian H = sum H_i where each H_i acts on at most k qubits, decide whether the smallest eigenvalue is below a or above b (with b - a >= 1/poly(n)). The bridge: a QMA verification circuit V is encoded into a 'history Hamiltonian' (Feynman-Kitaev clock construction) whose ground-state energy is small iff there is a witness V accepts. This makes physical ground-state estimation the canonical QMA-complete problem.
bridge.expected_bridge: QMA verifier circuit V -> history Hamiltonian H_V (sum of local clock + propagation + input + output terms); accepting witness for V <-> low-energy state of H_V. Functor between QMA decision problems and local-Hamiltonian eigenvalue problems.
bridge.source: Kitaev 1999 (Classical and Quantum Computation, Kitaev-Shen-Vyalyi 2002); Kempe-Kitaev-Regev 2006
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; the foundational bridge of Hamiltonian complexity.

bridge.probe: How does the recent MIP* = RE result connect a complexity-class equality to a question Connes asked about von Neumann algebras in 1976?
bridge.expected_answer: Ji-Natarajan-Vidick-Wright-Yuen (2020) proved MIP* = RE (multi-prover interactive proofs with quantum entanglement decide exactly the recursively enumerable languages). A consequence: there exist nonlocal games whose commuting-operator value strictly exceeds their tensor-product value, which by Tsirelson's theorem (and Ozawa's reformulation) implies that Connes' embedding conjecture (every separable type II_1 factor embeds into an ultrapower of the hyperfinite II_1 factor) is FALSE. So a complexity-class equality refutes a 44-year-old operator-algebra conjecture.
bridge.expected_bridge: Nonlocal games -> commuting-operator vs tensor-product correlations -> equivalence with Connes' conjecture (Tsirelson/Ozawa). MIP* = RE separates the two correlation types, refuting the embedding conjecture.
bridge.source: Ji-Natarajan-Vidick-Wright-Yuen 2020 (MIP*=RE); Tsirelson 1993; Ozawa 2013 (Connes' embedding conjecture and Tsirelson's problem)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream landmark; a rare proven bridge whose full chain crosses TCS, quantum information, and pure operator algebra.

bridge.probe: How does Impagliazzo-Wigderson's hardness-vs-randomness theorem turn a circuit lower bound into a derandomization?
bridge.expected_answer: Impagliazzo-Wigderson (1997) proved: if there exists a language in DTIME(2^O(n)) requiring circuits of size 2^Omega(n), then P = BPP. The proof builds a Nisan-Wigderson pseudorandom generator from a hard function: hardness of f against size-s circuits implies the NW generator with seed length O(log n) and output length n fools all size-s/2 circuits. The bridge: a worst-case lower bound on E against exponential-size circuits is converted (via Yao's hard-core lemma + NW) into a derandomization of polynomial-time randomized algorithms.
bridge.expected_bridge: Hard function f in E -> NW pseudorandom generator G_f with logarithmic seed; circuit size lower bound on f -> distinguishing advantage upper bound for G_f against small circuits; full derandomization of BPP.
bridge.source: Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997 (P = BPP if E requires exponential circuits); Nisan-Wigderson 1994

bridge.probe: In the information-complexity framework, what is the precise relation between the (internal) information cost of a protocol and its communication cost, and how is this used to prove direct-sum theorems?
bridge.expected_answer: Bar-Yossef-Jayram-Kumar-Sivakumar (2004), with refinements by Barak-Braverman-Chen-Rao (2013) and Braverman-Rao (2011), define the (internal) information complexity IC(f, mu) of a function f under input distribution mu as the minimum, over correct protocols, of I(X;Pi|Y) + I(Y;Pi|X). The basic relation is IC(f) <= CC(f) (a protocol of c bits leaks at most c bits of information). Conversely, communication can be compressed to roughly information cost (Braverman-Rao 2011: exponentially in some regimes). This gives direct-sum theorems: CC(f^n) >= n * IC(f) - o(n), since information cost is exactly additive.
bridge.expected_bridge: Protocols Pi -> random variables; communication cost = bit-length entropy bound; information cost = mutual information leaked. CC bounded below by IC; direct sums follow because IC is additive while CC need not be.
bridge.source: Bar-Yossef-Jayram-Kumar-Sivakumar 2004; Braverman-Rao 2011 (Information equals amortized communication); Braverman 2012
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; the formula IC <= CC and its converse compression are the standard explicit bridge.

bridge.probe: How does the Carmosino-Impagliazzo-Kabanets-Kolokolova theorem turn natural-proof circuit lower bounds into learning algorithms for the corresponding circuit class?
bridge.expected_answer: Carmosino-Impagliazzo-Kabanets-Kolokolova (2016) proved: a natural property useful against a circuit class C (in the Razborov-Rudich sense) implies a quasi-polynomial-time learning algorithm for C with membership queries under the uniform distribution. Concretely, the natural property's largeness/constructivity yields a distinguisher between random and pseudorandom strings, which by the Goldreich-Levin/NW-style transformation gives a Boolean-function compression algorithm, which then yields PAC learning for C. Combined with NW pseudorandom generators, this is the 'learning-from-natural-proofs' bridge.
bridge.expected_bridge: Natural property useful vs C -> distinguisher for C-pseudorandom strings -> compression algorithm for C-functions -> learning algorithm for C. A bidirectional functor between proof techniques and learning.
bridge.source: Carmosino-Impagliazzo-Kabanets-Kolokolova 2016 (Learning Algorithms from Natural Proofs); building on Razborov-Rudich 1997
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; the modern formal bridge between proof barriers and algorithm design.

bridge.probe: How does the PCP theorem reformulate NP-hardness of approximation as a statement about constraint-satisfaction and Fourier analysis on the Boolean cube?
bridge.expected_answer: The PCP theorem (Arora-Lund-Motwani-Sudan-Szegedy 1992; Dinur 2007) states NP = PCP(O(log n), O(1)): every NP language has a probabilistically checkable proof readable with O(log n) random bits and a constant number of queries. Equivalently, MAX-3SAT has no PTAS unless P = NP. The Hastad 1997 'optimal' tight inapproximability results (3SAT to within 7/8 + epsilon, 3LIN to within 1/2 + epsilon) are proved by analyzing long-code tests via Fourier analysis on F_2^n: soundness analysis decomposes a query test's acceptance probability into Fourier coefficients of the prover's encoded proof, and bounding the influence of large coefficients yields tight inapproximability.
bridge.domain_b: Fourier analysis on the Boolean cube / discrete harmonic analysis
bridge.expected_bridge: PCP verifiers -> long-code-encoded proofs -> tests on F_2^n; soundness = sum of squared Fourier coefficients weighted by test structure; Bonami-Beckner hypercontractivity bounds influence terms, giving tight inapproximability.
bridge.source: Arora-Lund-Motwani-Sudan-Szegedy 1992 (PCP theorem); Hastad 1997 (Some optimal inapproximability results); Dinur 2007 (PCP via gap amplification)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; Fourier-analytic PCP soundness is now standard.

bridge.probe: What is the explicit relation between the size of a Resolution refutation of an unsatisfiable CNF and the width of that refutation, and how is this a bridge between propositional proof systems and combinatorial pebbling?
bridge.expected_answer: Ben-Sasson and Wigderson (2001) proved Size >= 2^Omega((width - w_0)^2 / n), where w_0 is the initial width: any short Resolution refutation can be converted into one of small width, so width lower bounds imply size lower bounds. The bridge to combinatorics: width is captured by an adversarial pebbling/Prover-Delayer game on the formula, and width lower bounds for Tseitin formulas on expander graphs follow from expansion (combinatorial isoperimetry). Thus propositional-proof size lower bounds reduce to combinatorial expansion arguments.
bridge.expected_bridge: Resolution refutation -> width measure -> Prover-Delayer game on clauses -> expansion of underlying graph. Size lower-bounded by exp(width^2/n); width lower-bounded by combinatorial expansion.
bridge.source: Ben-Sasson-Wigderson 2001 (Short proofs are narrow); Haken 1985 (Pigeonhole); Urquhart 1987 (Tseitin expanders)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; size-width is the canonical proof-complexity / combinatorics bridge.

bridge.probe: How is the Waring rank of a homogeneous polynomial both an algebraic-complexity measure and an algebraic-geometry invariant, and what is the shared definition?
bridge.expected_answer: The Waring rank R_W(f) of a degree-d form f in n variables is the smallest r such that f = sum_{i=1}^r l_i^d for linear forms l_i. As an arithmetic-complexity measure, it is exactly the minimum size of a depth-3 sum-of-powers-of-linear-forms (Sigma-Pi-Sigma with top fan-in r) circuit for f, the basic restricted model in algebraic complexity. As an algebraic-geometry invariant, R_W(f) = min r such that [f] lies on the r-th secant variety sigma_r(nu_d(P^{n-1})) of the Veronese variety nu_d(P^{n-1}). Border Waring rank corresponds to the closure. Lower bounds come from Apolarity (Macaulay's inverse systems): R_W(f) >= dim of certain catalecticant matrices.
bridge.expected_bridge: Polynomial f -> point [f] in projective space; depth-3 power-sum circuit of size r <-> [f] lies on r-th secant of Veronese; same invariant computed two ways.
bridge.source: Sylvester (catalecticants, 1851); Iarrobino-Kanev 1999 (Power Sums, Gorenstein Algebras, and Determinantal Loci); Landsberg 2012

bridge.probe: How does the log-rank conjecture connect deterministic communication complexity of a Boolean function f(x,y) to the linear-algebraic rank of its communication matrix, and what is the current status?
bridge.expected_answer: The log-rank conjecture (Lovasz-Saks 1988) states that for every Boolean function f with communication matrix M_f over the reals, the deterministic communication complexity D(f) satisfies D(f) = (log rank(M_f))^{O(1)}. A trivial direction holds: D(f) >= log_2 rank(M_f) (any rectangle decomposition gives a low-rank covering). The polynomial-overhead converse is open. The best known: D(f) = O(sqrt(rank(M_f)) * log rank(M_f)) (Lovett 2016), a major improvement over earlier sqrt(rank) * log^2 rank bounds. Counterexamples to the log-rank conjecture for partial functions exist (Goos-Pitassi-Watson 2018), but the total-function case remains the central open problem.
bridge.expected_bridge: Boolean function f(x,y) -> communication matrix M_f over reals; rank(M_f) over reals -> conjecturally polylog-equivalent to deterministic communication complexity D(f).
bridge.source: Lovasz-Saks 1988 (Lattices, Mobius Functions, and Communication Complexity); Lovett 2016; Goos-Pitassi-Watson 2018
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; the most famous open conjecture in communication complexity.

bridge.probe: What is the Valiant-Vazirani isolation lemma's bridge from the structure of NP (existence of any witness) to the structure of UP (existence of a unique witness), and how does it use random hashing?
bridge.expected_answer: Valiant-Vazirani (1986) proved: NP is randomly reducible to USAT (Unique-SAT). Given a CNF phi with witness set W, pick a random hash h: {0,1}^n -> {0,1}^k for k uniform in [1,n]; with probability Omega(1/n), exactly one w in W satisfies h(w) = 0. So one can reduce SAT to a promise version where a yes-instance has exactly one satisfying assignment. The bridge sends the existential structure of NP to the unique-witness structure of UP via pairwise-independent hashing: a counting/hashing argument turns existence into uniqueness with non-trivial probability.
bridge.expected_bridge: SAT instance phi -> phi conjoined with random pairwise-independent hash constraints -> UNIQUE-SAT promise instance, with success probability Omega(1/n). Bridge from NP to UP via hashing.
bridge.source: Valiant-Vazirani 1986 (NP is as easy as detecting unique solutions)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; the canonical isolation/hashing bridge in complexity.

bridge.probe: What invariant of an algebraic branching program (ABP) for a polynomial f equals an invariant of a non-commutative formula or matrix factorization of f, providing a shared-invariant view of ABP-complexity?
bridge.expected_answer: Nisan (1991) proved that the minimum width of an algebraic branching program computing a non-commutative polynomial f equals the rank of a specific 'partial derivative / coefficient' matrix M_f^{(k)} whose rows and columns are indexed by monomials of length k and n-k, with entries the coefficient in f of their concatenation. Thus min-ABP-width = max_k rank(M_f^{(k)}) over the non-commutative ring. The same invariant has a representation-theoretic / matrix-factorization meaning: it is the rank of f viewed as a non-commutative bilinear form, equivalently the smallest r with f = u^T (A_1 x_1 + ... + A_n x_n)^d v expressible by r-dimensional matrices.
bridge.expected_bridge: Non-commutative polynomial f -> coefficient matrices M_f^{(k)}; min ABP width = max_k rank(M_f^{(k)}); same number as rank of non-commutative bilinear form / matrix factorization.
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: Mainstream; classical case-5 within algebraic complexity.
